text:
🎧 YouGlish
When calling someone and a third party answers, ask: {{c1::"Hi, is Colin there?"}}
explanation:
Native speed: 'haɪ, ɪz kɑ·lɪn ðɛr?'
Rules applied:
• is Colin there → /ɪz kɑ·lɪn ðɛr/ (linking consonant to consonant)

🧠 'Is [Name] there?' is the standard informal way to ask to speak to someone on the phone. It is friendlier than 'May I speak to [Name]?' which is reserved for formal customer service calls.
usage:
• "Hi, is Colin there? I'm returning his call."
  • "Hi, is Sarah there by any chance?"
spanish: 'Hola, ¿está Colin?' — Forma informal estándar para pedir hablar con alguien al teléfono. Es mucho más cercana que 'May I speak to...' (reservado para atención al cliente formal).

scenario: 📱 Phone and Messages — Bad Connection: 'You're breaking up'
text:
🎧 YouGlish
When the connection is bad and you can't hear the other person clearly, say: {{c1::"Hey, you're breaking up. Can you repeat that?"}}
explanation:
Native speed: 'heɪ, jər breɪ·kɪ·ŋʌp. kə·njuː rɪ·piːt ðæt?'
• breaking up → /breɪ·kɪ·ŋʌp/ (linking C+V: final /ŋ/ of 'breaking' links to 'up')
• Can you → /kə·njuː/ or /kə·njə/ (schwa reduction + palatal linking)

🧠 'Breaking up' is a specific phrasal verb meaning that the audio signal is cutting in and out. Compare with 'You cut out' (the audio stopped completely or the call dropped).
usage:
• "I'm losing you, you're breaking up. I'll call you back."
  • "You're breaking up a bit, let me move to another room."
spanish: 'Oye, te estás cortando. ¿Puedes repetir eso?' — Indicar una mala señal telefónica. 'Breaking up' se refiere a que la voz va y viene. Fonéticamente, 'breaking up' se une de forma continua y 'can' se reduce a schwa.
